definition: An exorcist expels or restrains a demon by invoking a higher authority — driving out an unclean spirit through sacred names and consecrated formulae. He is the conjurer's mirror image: both command spirits by adjuration, but the exorcist casts out where the conjurer calls in, and the Church authorizes his rite as clerical office rather than illicit art.
history: The word comes from Greek *exorkistēs*, from *exorkizein*, 'to bind by oath' (*ex-* plus *horkos*, 'oath'). The logic is that a demon is not overpowered but out-sworn — bound by a stronger oath in a stronger name. Kieckhefer notes Christian exorcists could command demons by using powerful and secret names, the name of Jesus foremost. The recurring turn was policing the boundary: as Trithemius insisted, the exorcist vested with power to expunge demons had to be told apart from the demon-conjuring sorcerer whose verbal technology looked almost identical. The word lands squarely on that contested line between sacrament and spell.

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

etymology.origin: Greek exorkistēs, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath' (ex- + horkos 'oath')
etymology.first: late 14c.; once a minor clerical order
etymology.note: You don't expel a demon by force but by a stronger oath — the same oath-logic as conjure and abjure.

flavor_snippets.html: Upon finding his warehouse infested, Gaven didn't know whether to get an exterminator or an <mark>exorcist</mark>.
Though they often bore little greater charm than the demons they battled, <mark>exorcists</mark> were always welcome in Scarwood.
